nodes.name: System Provenance
nodes.description: Enable any system — regardless of domain — to record where every part came from, what decisions shaped it, and how it reached its current form.

nodes.name: Layered Abstraction
description: A system is understood through distinct layers of abstraction: intent, concept, capability, structure, and realisation.
Each layer is valid independently of the layers below it.

nodes.name: Decision-Driven Evolution
description: Systems evolve through explicit decisions.
Decisions select between alternatives, affect nodes, and must preserve invariants.
Decisions are never deleted — they are superseded.

nodes.name: Append-Only History
description: The history of a system is additive. Nodes are deprecated or retired, not erased.
Changes record what happened; they do not overwrite prior state.

nodes.name: Recursive Composition
description: Any node may be treated as a subsystem with its own internal structure.
The same model applies at every level of nesting.

nodes.name: Process as Structure
description: Workflows, roles, stages, gates, and artefacts are modelled with the same rigour as domain concepts.
Process is not a second-class concern.

nodes.name: Format Agnosticism
description: The model defines semantics, not storage.
It may be represented in Markdown, JSON, a database, or any other structured format.

nodes.name: External Resource Handling
description: Nodes may relate to resources outside the graph.
Content may be internalised for portability or referenced for traceability, or both.

nodes.name: Conformance
nodes.description: A system conforms to SysProM if it meets a defined set of minimum requirements, ensuring interoperability and consistency.

nodes.name: Cross-Layer Traceability
nodes.description: Trace any node from intent through concept, capability, and structure to realisation.

nodes.name: Decision Recording
nodes.description: Record choices with alternatives considered, rationale, affected nodes, and preserved invariants.

nodes.name: Invariant Enforcement
description: Define rules that must hold across all valid system states.
Decisions and changes must explicitly identify which invariants they preserve.

nodes.name: Change Tracking
nodes.description: Record every addition, modification, removal, and transition with scope, decision, and lifecycle state.

nodes.name: Recursive Modelling
nodes.description: Represent any node as a subsystem with its own nodes, relationships, decisions, and changes, using the same conventions at every depth.

nodes.name: Process Modelling
nodes.description: Define protocols, stages, roles, gates, modes, artefacts, and artefact flows as first-class graph nodes.

nodes.name: Flexible Representation
nodes.description: Encode the model in a single file, a multi-document folder, nested folders, or a non-file format, with the same underlying semantics.

nodes.name: External Resource Referencing
nodes.description: Reference or internalise resources outside the graph with typed roles (input, output, context, evidence, source, standard, prior_art).

nodes.name: Separate What From Why From How
nodes.description: Intent, decisions, and realisations are distinct concerns and should be recorded separately.

nodes.name: Decisions Are More Important Than Documents
nodes.description: Documents describe state. Decisions explain why state changed.

nodes.name: Everything Has Identity
nodes.description: Every node gets an ID. No anonymous ideas.

nodes.name: Think Graph, Not Timeline
nodes.description: Timelines are one projection of the graph. The graph is the source of truth.

nodes.name: Separate State From History
nodes.description: What exists now and how it got there are recorded separately.

nodes.name: Concept Independence
nodes.description: Concept nodes MUST NOT depend on realisation nodes. The conceptual layer must be valid even if no realisation exists.

nodes.name: Decision-Change Linkage
nodes.description: Every change MUST reference at least one decision. No change occurs without an explicit choice.

nodes.name: Invariant Preservation
description: A decision that affects domain nodes (intent, concept, capability, element, invariant) MUST identify the invariants it preserves via must_preserve relationships.
A decision that affects only non-domain nodes (realisation, policy, protocol, stage, role, gate, mode, artefact) SHOULD identify preserved invariants but is not required to.
Domain nodes define what the system IS. Non-domain nodes define how it works or how it is implemented.

nodes.name: Recursive Consistency
description: The same model MUST apply at all levels of recursion.
A subsystem uses the same node types, relationship types, and conventions as the root system.

nodes.name: Append-Only History
nodes.description: Superseded decisions and deprecated nodes MUST remain part of the system. History is never deleted.

nodes.name: Node Identity
nodes.description: Every node MUST have a unique identifier and a defined type. No anonymous entities.

nodes.name: Relationship Validity
nodes.description: Every relationship MUST be directional, typed, and reference valid nodes.

nodes.name: Gate Justification
nodes.description: Every gate MUST reference the invariant or policy it enforces.

nodes.name: Layer Direction
nodes.description: Refinement flows downward through abstraction layers. Nodes MUST NOT refine nodes in a lower layer.

nodes.name: Realisation Implements Element
nodes.description: Realisation nodes MUST implement element nodes. A realisation without a parent element is invalid.

nodes.name: Stage Ordering
nodes.description: Stages within a protocol MUST have defined ordering via precedes or must_follow relationships.

nodes.name: Decision Affects Reference
nodes.description: Every decision MUST reference the nodes it affects via an affects relationship.

nodes.name: Decision Selection
nodes.description: Every decision MUST define the selected option and list alternatives considered.

nodes.name: Change Scope
nodes.description: Every change MUST define its scope (the nodes it affects).

nodes.name: Change Operations
nodes.description: Every change MUST define its operations (add, update, remove, link).

nodes.name: Change Lifecycle State
nodes.description: Every change MUST define its lifecycle state.

nodes.name: Node Addressability
nodes.description: Every node MUST be addressable — reachable by its identifier from any point in the graph.

nodes.name: Extension Constraint Preservation
nodes.description: Extensions (additional node types, relationship types, lifecycle states) MUST NOT violate core constraints.

nodes.name: External Reference Role Required
nodes.description: Every external reference MUST include a role describing the relationship to the node.

nodes.name: External Reference Directionality
description: External references are always from a node in the graph to a resource outside it.
External resources do not point back into the graph.

nodes.name: Prefer Deprecation Over Deletion
nodes.description: When removing a node from active use, mark it as deprecated rather than deleting it.

nodes.name: Decisions Must Record Alternatives
nodes.description: A decision SHOULD list the alternatives considered, not only the selected option.

nodes.name: Changes Must Define Scope
nodes.description: A change SHOULD explicitly state which nodes it affects and which it does not.

nodes.name: Capabilities Should Refine Concepts
nodes.description: Capability nodes SHOULD refine concept nodes.

nodes.name: Elements Should Realise Capabilities
nodes.description: Element nodes SHOULD realise capability nodes.

nodes.name: Prefer Internalisation for Portability
nodes.description: When portability matters, the relevant content from an external resource SHOULD be captured directly within a node so the document set is self-contained.

nodes.name: Security — Node Identity Integrity
nodes.description: Implementations SHOULD ensure integrity of node identities.

nodes.name: Security — Relationship Consistency
nodes.description: Implementations SHOULD ensure consistency of relationships.

nodes.name: Security — Controlled Modification
nodes.description: Implementations SHOULD ensure controlled modification of decisions and changes.

nodes.name: Conformance — Typed Nodes
nodes.description: A conformant system MUST define nodes with types.

nodes.name: Conformance — Relationships
nodes.description: A conformant system MUST define relationships between nodes.

nodes.name: Conformance — Lifecycle States
nodes.description: A conformant system MUST define lifecycle states for decisions and changes.

nodes.name: Conformance — At Least One Invariant
nodes.description: A conformant system MUST define at least one invariant.

nodes.name: Conformance — Traceability
nodes.description: A conformant system MUST support traceability across abstraction layers.

nodes.name: Domain Node Family
nodes.description: The set of node types that model what the system is.

nodes.name: intent
nodes.description: System purpose or goal. Stable, independent of implementation.

nodes.name: concept
nodes.description: Abstract model or idea. Defines what the system is, independent of structure and realisation.

nodes.name: capability
nodes.description: Enabled behaviour. Derived from concepts, independent of implementation.

nodes.name: element
nodes.description: Structural unit (logical part of system). Defines organisation, may depend on other elements.

nodes.name: realisation
nodes.description: Concrete implementation of an element. Multiple realisations may coexist (alternative, concurrent, or experimental).

nodes.name: invariant
nodes.description: Constraint that must always hold. Independent of decisions and changes. Constrains allowable system states.

nodes.name: Process Node Family
nodes.description: The set of node types that model how work flows through the system.

nodes.name: principle
nodes.description: Normative design value. May be overridden with justification, unlike invariants.

nodes.name: policy
nodes.description: Operational routing, gating, or selection rule. Implements principles and invariants.

nodes.name: protocol
nodes.description: Defined sequence of stages performed by roles. Protocols MAY depend on other protocols.

nodes.name: stage
nodes.description: A step within a protocol. MUST have defined ordering. MAY produce or consume artefacts.

nodes.name: role
nodes.description: A participant that performs stages. MAY be human, automated, or agent-based.

nodes.name: gate
description: Conditional blocker or redirector. MUST reference the invariant or policy it enforces.
MAY block a stage or route to an alternative.

nodes.name: mode
description: Named behavioural configuration that modifies a protocol or stage without redefining the system.
MAY be triggered by gates, selected by policies, or chosen explicitly.

nodes.name: Artefact Node Family
nodes.description: The set of node types that model what is produced and consumed.

nodes.name: artefact
nodes.description: A document, record, or output produced or consumed during system evolution.

nodes.name: artefact_flow
description: A transformation of one artefact into another within a stage.
Provides traceability of how documents and records evolve through a process.

nodes.name: Evolution Node Family
nodes.description: The set of node types that model how the system changes over time.

nodes.name: decision
description: Selection between alternatives that influences system structure or behaviour.
MUST define selected option, list alternatives, identify affected nodes, and identify preserved invariants.
MAY supersede prior decisions. Superseded decisions remain in history.

nodes.name: change
description: System modification over time.
MUST define scope, reference decisions, define operations (add/update/remove/link), and define lifecycle state.
MAY include execution plan. MAY overlap, depend on other changes, and be partially completed.

nodes.name: Projection Node Family
nodes.description: Optional node types for views and snapshots.

nodes.name: view
nodes.description: A named projection of a subset of the graph.

nodes.name: milestone
nodes.description: A named point in evolution.

nodes.name: version
nodes.description: A frozen snapshot of the system.

nodes.name: Relationship Type Registry
nodes.description: The set of typed, directed connections available between nodes.

nodes.name: refines
nodes.description: Abstraction refinement (e.g. capability refines concept).

nodes.name: realises
nodes.description: Fulfils a capability.

nodes.name: implements
nodes.description: Fulfils an element.

nodes.name: depends_on
nodes.description: Dependency between nodes.

nodes.name: constrained_by
nodes.description: Governed by an invariant.

nodes.name: affects
nodes.description: Impact of a decision or change on a node.

nodes.name: supersedes
nodes.description: Replacement of one node by another.

nodes.name: must_preserve
nodes.description: Preservation of an invariant by a decision or change.

nodes.name: performs
nodes.description: A role performs a stage.

nodes.name: part_of
nodes.description: A stage belongs to a protocol.

nodes.name: precedes
nodes.description: Ordering between stages.

nodes.name: must_follow
nodes.description: Strict ordering constraint between stages.

nodes.name: blocks
nodes.description: A gate blocks a stage.

nodes.name: routes_to
nodes.description: A gate redirects to a stage.

nodes.name: governed_by
nodes.description: A gate or policy is governed by a principle or invariant.

nodes.name: modifies
nodes.description: A mode modifies a protocol or stage.

nodes.name: triggered_by
nodes.description: A mode is triggered by a gate.

nodes.name: applies_to
nodes.description: A mode applies to a protocol.

nodes.name: produces
nodes.description: A stage produces an artefact.

nodes.name: consumes
nodes.description: A stage consumes an artefact.

nodes.name: transforms_into
nodes.description: An artefact flow transforms input to output.

nodes.name: selects
nodes.description: A decision selects a realisation.

nodes.name: requires
nodes.description: A gate or mode requires a condition.

nodes.name: disables
nodes.description: A mode disables a capability.

nodes.name: External Reference Model
nodes.description: The mechanism for relating nodes to resources outside the graph.

nodes.name: Internalisation
description: Content from an external resource captured directly within a node.
The node becomes self-contained and does not depend on the external source.

nodes.name: External Reference
nodes.description: A declared relationship to a resource outside the graph, identified by a serialisation-specific identifier with a typed role.

nodes.name: input
nodes.description: Resource that informed the creation of this node.

nodes.name: output
nodes.description: Resource produced as a result of this node.

nodes.name: context
nodes.description: Background material relevant to understanding this node.

nodes.name: evidence
nodes.description: Material that supports or justifies this node.

nodes.name: source
nodes.description: Original material from which this node was derived.

nodes.name: standard
nodes.description: External standard or specification that this node conforms to or enforces.

nodes.name: prior_art
nodes.description: Existing work that this node relates to or was influenced by.

nodes.name: Decision Lifecycle
nodes.description: The lifecycle state machine for decision nodes.

nodes.name: proposed
nodes.description: Decision has been proposed but not yet evaluated.

nodes.name: accepted
nodes.description: Decision has been accepted as the chosen path.

nodes.name: implemented
nodes.description: Decision has been implemented in the system.

nodes.name: adopted
nodes.description: Decision has been fully adopted across the system.

nodes.name: superseded
nodes.description: Decision has been replaced by a newer decision.

nodes.name: abandoned
nodes.description: Decision was accepted but later abandoned without implementation.

nodes.name: deferred
nodes.description: Decision was accepted but deferred for later implementation.

nodes.name: Change Lifecycle
nodes.description: The lifecycle state machine for change nodes.

nodes.name: defined
nodes.description: Change has been defined with scope and operations.

nodes.name: introduced
nodes.description: Change has been introduced into the system.

nodes.name: in_progress
nodes.description: Change is being actively worked on.

nodes.name: complete
nodes.description: Change has been fully applied.

nodes.name: consolidated
nodes.description: Change has been consolidated and cleaned up.

nodes.name: Node Lifecycle
nodes.description: The general lifecycle state machine for nodes.

nodes.name: proposed
nodes.description: Node has been proposed but is not yet active.

nodes.name: active
nodes.description: Node is currently active in the system.

nodes.name: deprecated
nodes.description: Node is deprecated but still present in the system.

nodes.name: retired
nodes.description: Node has been retired from active use.

nodes.name: File Representation
nodes.description: Conventions for encoding SysProM in file-based formats.

nodes.name: Root Entry Point Identification
description: The root entry point MUST be identifiable as a SysProM document.
Valid filenames include SysProM.md, SYSPROM.md, SPM.md, and README.spm.md.
Alternatively, front matter containing doc_type: sysprom is sufficient.

nodes.name: Root Entry Point Location
description: A SysProM document set MAY live at any location.
Valid locations include: repository root (./SysProM.md), dedicated folder (./SysProM/README.md), docs directory (./docs/SysProM.md), or any other reasonable path.

nodes.name: Multi-Document Hub
description: When a system is described across multiple files, a hub document (typically README.md) SHOULD serve as the root node.
Separate files per concern: INTENT.md, INVARIANTS.md, STATE.md, DECISIONS.md, CHANGES.md.
No document is mandatory.

nodes.name: Single-File Node Extension
description: A node represented as a single file SHOULD use the .spm.md extension.
Filename SHOULD include node ID and MAY include name: F1-sync.spm.md (preferred), F1.spm.md, or sync.spm.md.

nodes.name: Folder Node Naming
description: A node represented as a folder SHOULD be named using the node ID and name: F1-sync/ (preferred), F1/, or sync/.
The folder MUST contain at least a README. md.

nodes.name: Grouping Folders
description: Node folders MAY be grouped under intermediate directories by type (e. g. features/, components/).
Grouping folders are organisational and do not represent nodes.

nodes.name: Parent Linking Implicit
description: Parent-child relationships between nodes are implicit from the folder hierarchy.
Explicit parent references in frontmatter are not required.

nodes.name: Relationship Notation Flexibility
description: Relationships MAY be expressed as labelled lists, arrow chains, tables, nested lists, or mermaid diagrams.
Formats may be mixed. The choice is a presentation concern.

nodes.name: Frontmatter Is Metadata Only
description: Front matter defines document-level metadata only (title, doc_type, scope, status, version).
Node-level data belongs in the document body.

nodes.name: Non-Linear Evolution
nodes.description: The model supports branching, merging, and revival of nodes.

nodes.name: Branching
nodes.description: The model MUST support multiple concurrent evolution paths (e.g. experimental branches).

nodes.name: Merging
nodes.description: Branches MAY be merged.

nodes.name: Revival
nodes.description: Previously deprecated or retired nodes MAY be reintroduced.

nodes.name: Extensibility
nodes.description: The mechanism for extending SysProM beyond its core types.

nodes.name: Markdown Representation
nodes.description: Primary representation using headings as nodes, lists as relationships, checkboxes as lifecycle, front matter as document metadata.

nodes.name: Single-File Form
nodes.description: All sections in one file (SysProM.md, SYSPROM.md, SPM.md, or README.spm.md).

nodes.name: Multi-Document Form
nodes.description: Separate files per concern (README, INTENT, INVARIANTS, STATE, DECISIONS, CHANGES).

nodes.name: Recursive Folder Form
nodes.description: Node folders with their own document sets. Nodes may also be single files using .spm.md extension.

nodes.name: JSON Serialisation
nodes.description: JSON representation validated against schema.json. Supports recursive composition via the subsystem property.

nodes.name: Separate Domain From Process From Evolution
context: The model needs to represent systems, workflows, and history.
Mixing these concerns makes the graph hard to query and reason about.

options.description: Single flat node type for everything
Typed nodes without family grouping
Nodes grouped into domain, process, artefact, and evolution families

rationale: Grouping into families enforces separation of concerns.
Domain structure should not be tangled with process mechanics or evolution history.

nodes.name: Make Decisions First-Class Entities
nodes.context: Systems evolve through choices, but most models bury decisions in prose or lose them entirely.

options.description: Embed decisions in prose within documents
Record decisions as standalone log entries
Model decisions as typed graph nodes with relationships to affected nodes and preserved invariants

rationale: Decisions are the causal mechanism of system evolution.
Graph nodes with typed relationships make decisions queryable, traceable, and enforceable.

nodes.name: Distinguish Invariants From Principles From Policies
nodes.context: Systems have rules at different levels of rigidity. Collapsing all rules into one type loses critical information.

options.description: Single constraint type for all rules
Two types: hard invariant and soft guideline
Three types: invariant (must always hold), principle (normative value, may be overridden), policy (operational rule)

nodes.rationale: The three-way split matches how real systems distinguish structural guarantees from design values from operational rules.

nodes.name: Add Process Modelling
nodes.context: Workflow-heavy systems (planning tools, spec-driven workflows, runtime orchestration) cannot be adequately modelled with decisions and changes alone.

options.description: Model process implicitly through decisions and changes only
Add roles and stages but not gates, modes, or artefacts
Add full process modelling: roles, protocols, stages, gates, policies, modes, artefacts, artefact flows

rationale: Without process nodes, roles become invisible, gates become buried in decision prose, and artefact lineage is lost.
Full process modelling makes the model capable of encoding systems like Spec Kit, Ralplan, and get-shit-done.

nodes.name: Format-Agnostic With Markdown as Primary Representation
nodes.context: The model needs a practical encoding but should not be locked to one format.

options.description: Define a single mandatory format (e.g. JSON schema)
Define semantics only, with no reference representation
Define semantics as normative, with Markdown as the primary practical representation

rationale: Markdown is human-readable, Git-friendly, renders on GitHub/GitLab, and supports front matter, headings, links, and checkboxes which map directly to model concepts.

nodes.name: Recursive Composition Using Same Conventions
nodes.context: Systems contain subsystems. If subsystems use different conventions, the model fractures.

options.description: Flat structure only (no nesting)
Nesting with different conventions at each level
Same document set, naming, and relationship conventions at every level of nesting

nodes.rationale: Recursive consistency means any node can be understood in isolation using the same patterns, and tools can process any level without special cases.

nodes.name: Append-Only History
nodes.context: Deleting history destroys traceability. If a decision is removed, it becomes impossible to answer why a node exists.

options.description: Allow deletion of nodes and decisions
Allow overwriting of decisions with new versions
Append-only: superseded decisions and deprecated nodes remain; nothing is deleted

nodes.rationale: Append-only preserves the full provenance chain.

nodes.name: Support External Resources via Reference and Internalisation
context: Nodes often relate to resources outside the graph.
The model must handle this without coupling to a specific serialisation format.

options.description: No external reference support — all content must be internalised
External references only — all content is by pointer
Both approaches supported independently or together, with serialisation-specific identifiers and typed roles

rationale: Internalisation enables portability. References enable traceability.
Supporting both gives implementors flexibility without losing either property.

nodes.name: Initial Model
nodes.description: Establishes the core domain model with layered abstraction, decisions, changes, and invariants.

nodes.name: Add Process Modelling
nodes.description: Extends the model with process, artefact, and projection node families.

nodes.name: Add File Representation Conventions
nodes.description: Defines how SysProM may be encoded in files, including single-document, multi-document, and recursive folder forms.

nodes.name: Add External Resources Model
nodes.description: Adds the external reference and internalisation mechanism with typed roles.

nodes.name: Add Lifecycle Protocols
nodes.description: Adds the decision, change, and node lifecycle state machines as protocols with stages and ordering.

nodes.name: Encode Full Normative Specification
nodes.description: Adds conformance requirements, missing invariants, security and extensibility policies, non-linear evolution capabilities, and complete node/relationship type vocabularies to make the JSON self-contained.

nodes.name: Text Field Duality
description: Fields that carry human-readable content (description, context, rationale, internalised) MAY be either a string or an array of strings.
Both forms are semantically equivalent.
Implementations MUST accept either form wherever a text field appears.

nodes.name: Allow Array-of-Lines for Text Fields
context: JSON does not support multiline strings.
Long descriptions serialised as single strings with embedded \n are hard to read and produce poor diffs.
An array of lines preserves readability in serialised form.

options.description: Single string only — use \n for newlines
Array of strings only — always use arrays
Either form accepted — string for short content, array for multiline

rationale: Short descriptions gain nothing from being wrapped in an array.
Long descriptions gain readability and diff quality from line-per-element arrays.
Accepting both avoids forcing a style while enabling better ergonomics where it matters.

nodes.name: Add Text Field Duality
description: Adds support for text fields (description, context, rationale, internalised) to accept either a string or an array of strings.
Updates the JSON schema, Zod definitions, and specification.

operations.description: JSON schema updated to accept string | string[] for text fields

nodes.name: Strict Type Enums
description: Node types, node statuses, relationship types, and external reference roles MUST be members of their defined enums.
Unknown values MUST be rejected at validation time.
Extensions MUST add new values to the enum rather than bypassing it.

nodes.name: Use Strict Enums for Core Types
context: SysProM's purpose is provenance and traceability.
If relationship types, node types, and statuses are arbitrary strings, layer constraints cannot be enforced, labels cannot be derived for rendering, and semantic validation is impossible.
The extensibility section says extensions MUST NOT violate core constraints, but an open string type makes that unenforceable.

options.description: Open strings with examples — any value accepted, core types documented only
Strict enums — only declared values accepted, extensions must add to the enum

rationale: Strict enums enable schema-level validation, type-safe label derivation, and enforceable layer constraints.
The DRY labelledEnum pattern ensures each type is defined once with its label, eliminating duplication.

nodes.name: Switch to Strict Enums with Labelled Definitions
description: Replaces open z.string() types with z.enum() for node types, statuses, relationship types, and external reference roles.
Introduces labelledEnum() helper that defines values and labels in one place.
Derives SECTION_LABELS, RELATIONSHIP_LABELS, and reverse lookups from the label maps.

operations.description: JSON schema now uses enum instead of string with examples

nodes.name: README Links Only to Present Files
nodes.description: Generated README navigation and document roles MUST only link to files that contain nodes. Dead links to absent files MUST NOT be generated.

nodes.name: Only Link to Present Files in README
context: The README generator was producing navigation links and document role entries for all possible files (INTENT, INVARIANTS, STATE, DECISIONS, CHANGES) regardless of whether the subsystem had nodes of those types.
This created dead links in subsystem READMEs.

options.description: Always link to all files — accept dead links as informational
Only link to files that will be created based on present node types

nodes.rationale: Dead links mislead readers and break tooling. Links should reflect reality.

nodes.name: Remove Navigation and Document Roles from README
context: The README contained a Navigation section and a Document Roles table that restated what the filenames already communicate.
Anyone looking at a folder with INTENT.md, DECISIONS.md, etc. already knows what they contain.

options.description: Keep both Navigation and Document Roles
Keep Document Roles table only
Remove both — the filenames are self-documenting

nodes.rationale: Removing redundant sections reduces noise and maintenance burden. The file naming convention is the documentation.

nodes.name: Fix Dead Links in Subsystem READMEs
nodes.description: README generator now only links to files that contain nodes for the given subsystem.

operations.description: json-to-md updated to check present node types before generating links

nodes.name: Remove Navigation and Document Roles from README
nodes.description: Removes the Navigation section and Document Roles table from generated READMEs. The file naming convention is self-documenting.

operations.description: README generation simplified to omit navigation and document roles

nodes.name: Layer-Dependent Invariant Preservation
context: INV3 required every decision to identify preserved invariants.
Operational decisions (naming, tooling, presentation) genuinely have no invariants at risk.
Forcing must_preserve on these creates friction that discourages recording decisions, which defeats SysProM's provenance purpose.
SysProM already distinguishes domain nodes (intent, concept, capability, element, invariant) from non-domain nodes (realisation, policy, protocol, etc.).

options.description: Keep INV3 as MUST for all decisions
Soften INV3 to SHOULD for all decisions
Add decision type/level classification
MUST when affecting domain nodes, SHOULD when affecting only non-domain nodes — determined automatically from affects relationships

rationale: Automatic classification from affects relationships means no user burden.
Domain nodes define what the system IS — decisions affecting them must consider invariants.
Non-domain nodes define how the system works — decisions affecting only these are operational.
Leverages the existing layer model rather than adding new concepts.

nodes.name: Make Invariant Preservation Layer-Dependent
description: Updates INV3 to require must_preserve only when a decision affects domain nodes.
Decisions affecting only non-domain nodes (realisations, policies, process nodes) should but are not required to identify preserved invariants.

operations.description: Reworded to distinguish domain and non-domain affects

nodes.name: System Comparisons
nodes.description: Comparison of SysProM against existing systems and tools, demonstrating what each covers and where SysProM fills gaps.

nodes.name: ADR Comparison
description: Architecture Decision Records cover decision history only.
SysProM subsumes ADR and adds structure, evolution, invariants, and graph-native traceability.

nodes.name: C4 Comparison
description: C4 covers multi-level structure (Context, Container, Component, Code).
It is static — no decisions, changes, or time. SysProM adds all three.

nodes.name: DDD Comparison
description: Domain-Driven Design covers intent and concept layers via ubiquitous language and bounded contexts.
Weak on decisions and evolution. SysProM adds both.

nodes.name: Event Sourcing Comparison
description: Event sourcing tracks changes as first-class events.
No abstraction layers, no decisions (why), no semantic structure. SysProM adds all three.

nodes.name: Knowledge Graph Comparison
description: Knowledge graphs provide the underlying data model (nodes + typed edges).
Lack lifecycle, decision semantics, and temporal workflow. SysProM adds these.

nodes.name: Traceability Matrix Comparison
description: Traceability matrices link requirements to design to implementation.
Very linear, no decisions, no non-linearity. SysProM is graph-native.

nodes.name: Enterprise Architecture Comparison
description: ArchiMate/TOGAF cover capability and structure layers with standardised elements.
Heavyweight, static, weak on iteration. SysProM is lightweight and decision-driven.

nodes.name: RFC Process Comparison
description: RFC processes cover decision lifecycle (proposal, discussion, acceptance).
Not connected to a system model. SysProM integrates decisions with the system graph.

nodes.name: Git Comparison
description: Git provides temporal mechanics (append-only history, branching, merging).
No semantic structure — just file diffs. SysProM adds typed nodes and relationships.

nodes.name: MBSE/SysML Comparison
description: MBSE is the closest formal equivalent — requirements, structure, behaviour, constraints.
SysProM can be understood as a lightweight, human-readable, decision-centric MBSE.

nodes.name: Spec Kit Comparison
description: Spec Kit is a spec-driven development toolkit where specifications become executable.
Can be fully modelled within SysProM as a protocol with stages, artefacts, and artefact flows.
SysProM adds invariants, decision supersession, and graph-native traceability.

nodes.name: Ralplan Comparison
description: Ralplan runs Planner, Architect, and Critic roles sequentially until consensus.
Its process can be modelled as a SysProM protocol with roles, stages, gates, and modes.
SysProM can store Ralplan's outputs but cannot replace the planning intelligence itself.

nodes.name: GSD Comparison
description: get-shit-done presents a simple command surface while hiding orchestration complexity.
Can be modelled as a system with multiple runtime realisations governed by a user-surface stability principle.
SysProM captures the architectural truth: one stable capability surface, multiple interchangeable realisations.

nodes.name: Summary
description: ADR=decisions, C4=structure, DDD=concepts, Event sourcing=changes, Knowledge graph=relationships, Traceability=linking, EA=layers, RFC=lifecycle, Git=history, MBSE=formal modelling.
Each optimises one axis. SysProM combines all into a single recursive graph.
Spec Kit, Ralplan, and GSD compose with SysProM: Ralplan generates plans, Spec Kit stores specs, GSD executes, SysProM tracks provenance.

nodes.name: Document Workspace Example
nodes.description: A worked example demonstrating SysProM applied to a document conversion webapp with placement-agnostic contracts and conditional sync constraints.

nodes.name: Document Workspace
nodes.description: Enable users to ingest, transform, store, and access documents consistently across contexts.

nodes.name: Document Transformation
nodes.description: Documents can be converted between representations.

nodes.name: Document Persistence
nodes.description: Documents can be stored and later retrieved.

nodes.name: Document Synchronisation
nodes.description: Documents can be kept consistent across multiple contexts.

nodes.name: Stable Document Identity
nodes.description: A document retains identity regardless of storage location.

nodes.name: Placement-Agnostic Conversion
nodes.description: The conversion contract is identical regardless of execution location.

nodes.name: Placement-Agnostic Storage
nodes.description: The storage contract is identical regardless of persistence location.

nodes.name: Sync Requires Shared Persistence
nodes.description: If synchronisation is enabled, persistence must support shared remote state.

nodes.name: Abstract Conversion Placement

options.description: UI distinguishes local and remote
Single contract independent of placement

nodes.rationale: Execution location is a realisation concern.

nodes.name: Planning Workflow Example
nodes.description: A worked example demonstrating SysProM process modelling with roles, protocols, stages, gates, modes, and artefact flows.

nodes.name: Reliable Change Delivery
nodes.description: Enable work to move from idea to execution in a controlled, reviewable, and traceable way.

nodes.name: Traceable Scope
nodes.description: No change may be executed unless its scope can be identified.

nodes.name: Review Before Approval
nodes.description: Approval cannot occur until review has completed.

nodes.name: Planner
nodes.description: Produces an initial scoped plan.

nodes.name: Architect
nodes.description: Reviews structural and design soundness.

nodes.name: Critic
nodes.description: Evaluates quality, completeness, and testability.

nodes.name: Scope Gate
nodes.description: Blocks execution when work is vague or underspecified.

nodes.name: Interactive Mode
nodes.description: Adds explicit user checkpoints.

nodes.name: Deliberate Mode
nodes.description: Adds stronger risk analysis and broader verification.

nodes.name: Internalise Design Archive into SysProM JSON
context: The distilled/ folder contained four reference documents (Specification, Comparisons, Examples, Naming) produced during SysProM's design.
These were external to the JSON but contained valuable content.
The specification is already captured as the JSON itself.
The comparisons, examples, and naming rationale could be modelled as artefact nodes with subsystems.

options.description: Keep distilled/ as separate files, reference from JSON
Internalise key content as artefact nodes with subsystems in the JSON

nodes.rationale: Internalising makes the JSON self-contained. Artefact nodes with subsystems naturally model documents that contain structured content.

nodes.name: Internalise Design Archive
description: Internalises content from distilled/ into the SysProM JSON.
Comparisons become an artefact node (ART1) with comparison summaries as subsystem concepts.
Worked examples become artefact nodes (ART2, ART3) with example SysProM graphs as subsystems.
Naming rationale is already captured in D8.
Specification is already captured as the JSON itself — distilled/Specification.md is redundant.

nodes.name: Subsystem Representation Heuristic
description: A subsystem is rendered as a single .spm.md file when it would produce only one document file type AND the result is 100 lines or fewer.
A subsystem is rendered as a multi-document folder when it would produce multiple file types OR the single file would exceed 100 lines.
Subsystems of the same node type are automatically grouped into a type-named directory when 2 or more exist.

nodes.name: Size-Based Subsystem Splitting
context: Small subsystems (e.g. 6 node type definitions) are cleaner as single .spm.md files.
Large subsystems (e.g. 24 relationship type definitions at 107 lines) become unwieldy in a single file.
The file type count heuristic alone doesn't catch single-type subsystems that are too long.

options.description: Split only by file type count — single type always stays as one file
Split by file type count AND line count — single type files over 100 lines become folders

nodes.rationale: Combining both heuristics keeps small subsystems compact while splitting large ones for readability.

nodes.name: Add Size-Based Subsystem Splitting and Auto-Grouping
description: Subsystems that would produce a single file over 100 lines are now split into multi-document folders.
Subsystems of the same node type are automatically grouped into type-named directories (e.g. elements/, artefacts/).
Both heuristics are automatic — no user configuration needed.

operations.description: json-to-md updated with line count threshold and auto-grouping

relationships.description: Comparisons informed the decision to add process modelling

external_references.description: Original design conversation that produced the SysProM model.
Spec Kit's workflow limitations motivated the addition of process modelling.
Ralplan's consensus planning protocol demonstrated the need for roles, stages, and gates as first-class nodes.
GSD's multi-runtime orchestration demonstrated the need for modes and runtime-adaptive realisations.
RFC 2119 defines the interpretation of MUST, SHOULD, and MAY used throughout the specification.
